title: try / catch / throw
successMessage: The second divide threw — execution jumped to catch, skipping 'Never reached'. After catch, the program continues normally. e.what() returns the error message string.

title: Multiple catch blocks
instruction:
Catch different exception types differently. Catch from most specific to most general.

Catch `...` (three dots) catches anything:
expectedOutput: Out of range: Index too large
successMessage: Catch from most specific to most general — invalid_argument and out_of_range before runtime_error (they both inherit from it). Catch (...) is the safety net for anything else.

title: Custom exception classes
successMessage: Custom exception with extra data (shortfall). The catch block accesses e.getShortfall() — more information than a generic error. Custom exceptions make error handling meaningful.

title: noexcept — promise to never throw
instruction:
`noexcept` is a function specifier promising it never throws.

The compiler can optimise `noexcept` functions more aggressively. If a `noexcept` function does throw, `std::terminate()` is called.
successMessage: noexcept enables move semantics optimisations — vectors use move instead of copy when they know the element's move constructor is noexcept. Mark destructors and swap as noexcept — always.

title: Exception safety and RAII
instruction:
RAII makes exception safety automatic — destructors run during stack unwinding, releasing resources.

No `try/catch` needed for resource cleanup when using RAII types.
successMessage: Lock released even though an exception was thrown! RAII makes this automatic. mutex locks, file handles, database connections — all released safely during exception unwinding. No try/catch needed for cleanup.

title: Build a safe calculator
instruction:
No starter code.

Create:
- `class MathError : public runtime_error` with an error code
- `class DivisionByZeroError : public MathError`
- `class OverflowError : public MathError`

Write `int safeCalculate(int a, string op, int b)` that:
- Throws `DivisionByZeroError` for `/` or `%` with b==0
- Throws `OverflowError` if the result would overflow int
- Handles `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`

Test with 5 operations including error cases.
hint: For overflow check: if (op == "+" && (long long)a + b > INT_MAX) throw OverflowError(...);
successMessage: A hierarchical exception system. Catching MathError catches both DivisionByZeroError and OverflowError. This is how production error handling works — catch at the right level of specificity. Chapter 18 done!
